sourceMovie.title: Cannibal Holocaust
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Cannibal Holocaust works as a horror movie with a point of view—fans quote moments, but they also argue about what the film is actually saying about power, desire, or survival. The film leans hard into its core genre, which is exactly what fans say they wanted more of from blockbusters in that era.

recommendations.title: Friday the 13th
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Cannibal Holocaust fans keep landing on Friday the 13th (1980) — the horror is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Wrong Turn
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Wrong Turn draws from the same horror well as Cannibal Holocaust, channeling thriller in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 2003 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: The Last House on the Left
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Last House on the Left (2009) captures the horror appeal of Cannibal Holocaust without simply imitating it. The crime streak running through both films is what puts The Last House on the Left at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

recommendations.title: Grindhouse
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of Cannibal Holocaust — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into Grindhouse (2007). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: The Taking of Deborah Logan
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind The Taking of Deborah Logan (2014) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps Cannibal Holocaust rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: V/H/S/2
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: V/H/S/2 (2013) is the kind of film Cannibal Holocaust fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: Rogue
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Rogue shares Cannibal Holocaust's gift for horror — the 2007 film delivers action without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Poughkeepsie Tapes
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes Cannibal Holocaust compelling — the horror, the mystery — shows up in The Poughkeepsie Tapes (2007) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: The Serpent and the Rainbow
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of Cannibal Holocaust is alive in The Serpent and the Rainbow (1988), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: Visitor Q
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Visitor Q (2001) operates in the same horror as Cannibal Holocaust, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

faq.question: What should I watch after Cannibal Holocaust?
faq.answer: Try Friday the 13th, Wrong Turn, The Last House on the Left—each shares DNA with Cannibal Holocaust while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Cannibal Holocaust?
faq.answer: Cannibal Holocaust (1980) is best described as Horror. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Cannibal Holocaust worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 6.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this horror lane.
